coredata.dc:description: Electrocatalytic nitrate reduction reaction (NO3RR) has gained great attention as a means of transforming environmental waste into fertilizers. However, the activity and efficiency of NO3RR is severely hindered by the inevitable self-coupling of active hydrogen (H*ads) competing with nitrate (NO3 -) hydrogenation. Here, an efficient electrocatalyst is developed by combining multifunction carbon dots (CDs) with bornite (Cu5FeS4) towards the comprehensive manipulation of the adsorption and conversion of H*ads. As a result, the optimized CDs/Cu5FeS4 presents a remarkable NH3 yield of 215.19 μmol h−1 cm−2 with a Faradaic efficiency of 86.52 %. Importantly, the introduction of CDs can greatly increase the NH3 yield and FE compared with pure Cu5FeS4, even under a wide range of NO3 - concentrations. Meanwhile, the catalyst can successfully transform simulated NO3 - groundwater into NH3 by using a flow cell. The cyclic voltammetry and electron paramagnetic resonance tests indicate that CDs effectively promote the production of H*ads and participate in NO3 - hydrogenation. Kinetic isotope experiments reveal the role of CDs in accelerating water dissociation and proton transfer processes. In-situ Raman spectroscopy demonstrate that CDs effectively boosts the adsorption and conversion of NO3 - to NO2 - and NO2 - to NH3. And DFT calculations reveal CDs significantly reduce the energy barrier of the key rate-determining step (*NO3 to *NO2), which thus promotes the hydrogenation reaction between H*ads and N-containing intermediates. This work provides an interesting insight for enhancing the catalytic activity of ammonia by utilizing CDs to promote the generation and conversion of H*ads.

(g) XRD and (h) Raman spectra of the samples. High-resolution XPS spectra of (i) Cu 2p and (j) Fe 2p for Cu5FeS4 and CDs/Cu5FeS4. Insets in (b-d): size distribution of the CDs, Cu5FeS4 and CDs/Cu5FeS4.

(a) LSV curves of CDs, Cu5FeS4 and CDs/Cu5FeS4. (b) NO3 - conversion, (c) NH3 yield and (d) FE of Cu5FeS4 and CDs/Cu5FeS4. (e) NH3 yield and FE under different NO3 - concentrations and (f) durability tests of CDs/Cu5FeS4 at −1.2 V vs. RHE.

(a) LSV curves and (b) NH3 yield of CDs/Cu5FeS4 in 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte (with and without NO3 -). 1H NMR spectra after NO3RR at different time using (c) 14NO3 - and (d) 15NO3 - as the N source. (e) The standard curve of integral area (15NH4 +/C4H4O4) against 15NH4 + concentration. (f) NH3 yield of CDs/Cu5FeS4 by Uv-vis, NMR-14N and NMR-15N methods after electrolysis for 1 hour at –1.2 V.

(e) NH3 yield and (f) NO3 - conversion ratio of CDs/Cu5FeS4 depending on the reaction time. (g) LSV curves, (h) NH3 yield and FE and (i) stability test of CDs/Cu5FeS4 in simulated groundwater under flow cell.

(c) CV curves of Cu5FeS4 and CDs/Cu5FeS4 in 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. (d) CV curves of CDs/Cu5FeS4 with and without NO3 -. (e) LSV curves of CDs/Cu5FeS4 with or without TBA as the quencher of H*. (f) ESR spectra of the captured H* by DMPO in the electrolyte after 10 min electrocatalysis of Cu5FeS4 and CDs/Cu5FeS4. (g) LSV curves of CDs/Cu5FeS4 at different ratios of H2O/D2O as the solvent. (h) KIE of H/D over Cu5FeS4 and CDs/Cu5FeS4.

In-situ Raman spectra of (a) Cu5FeS4 and (b) CDs/Cu5FeS4 during NO3RR at different potentials. (c) Free energy diagram for NO3RR over Cu5FeS4 and CDs/Cu5FeS4. d) Schematic diagram of the catalytic mechanism of NO3RR.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-boosted active hydrogen for efficient electrocatalytic reduction of nitrate to ammonia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrocatalytic nitrate reduction reaction (NO3RR) has gained great attention as a means of transforming environmental waste into fertilizers. However, the activity and efficiency of NO3RR is severely hindered by the inevitable self-coupling of active hydrogen (H*ads) competing with nitrate (NO3 -) hydrogenation. Here, an efficient electrocatalyst is developed by combining multifunction carbon dots (CDs) with bornite (Cu5FeS4) towards the comprehensive manipulation of the adsorption and conversion of H*ads. As a result, the optimized CDs/Cu5FeS4 presents a remarkable NH3 yield of 215.19 μmol h−1 cm−2 with a Faradaic efficiency of 86.52 %. Importantly, the introduction of CDs can greatly increase the NH3 yield and FE compared with pure Cu5FeS4, even under a wide range of NO3 - concentrations. Meanwhile, the catalyst can successfully transform simulated NO3 - groundwater into NH3 by using a flow cell. The cyclic voltammetry and electron paramagnetic resonance tests indicate that CDs effectively promote the production of H*ads and participate in NO3 - hydrogenation. Kinetic isotope experiments reveal the role of CDs in accelerating water dissociation and proton transfer processes. In-situ Raman spectroscopy demonstrate that CDs effectively boosts the adsorption and conversion of NO3 - to NO2 - and NO2 - to NH3. And DFT calculations reveal CDs significantly reduce the energy barrier of the key rate-determining step (*NO3 to *NO2), which thus promotes the hydrogenation reaction between H*ads and N-containing intermediates. This work provides an interesting insight for enhancing the catalytic activity of ammonia by utilizing CDs to promote the generation and conversion of H*ads.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional CDs modified Cu5FeS4 directly reducing nitrate and nitrate groundwater to ammonia by comprehensively manipulating the adsorption and conversion of H*ads.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia (NH3), as a carbon-free energy carrier and chemical, plays an important role in the future energy system due to its high energy density (4.32 kWh L−1) and hydrogen capacity (17.65 %) [1,2]. At present, the synthesis of NH3 mainly comes from the traditional Haber–Bosch process that involves the reluctant reaction between nitrogen (N2) and hydrogen under high temperature (400–500 ℃) and high pressure (150–300 atm) [3,4]. Thus, it is imperative to develop green and sustainable methods to replace the traditional methods [5]. Electrocatalytic N2 reduction has received more attention due to it can realize the conversion from N2 to NH3 at room conditions, but the weak solubility and strong triple bond (NN, 941 kJ mol−1) of N2 lead to low catalytic activity and Faraday efficiency (FE) [6–9]. Compared with N2 molecules, nitrate (NO3 -) has strong solubility in aqueous solution and relatively low dissociation energy of N = O (204 kJ mol−1). More importantly, NO3 - is also an environmental pollutant that widely exists in industrial wastewater and groundwater [10,11]. Therefore, NO3 - reduction reaction (NO3RR) not only generates high-value NH3 and other nitrogen-based fertilizers at ambient and temperature pressure, but also solves the problem of environmental pollution [12,13]. However, NO3 --to-NH3 conversion is a complex multiple-electron/proton transfer process, in which several side reactions seriously hinder the selectivity and yield of NH3 [14–16]. Thereby, the design of catalysts mainly focuses on high activity and high selectivity.

At present, a significant amount of work is devoted to improving NH3 yield and FE in high concentrations of NO3 -, while neglecting the research on different concentrations of NO3 -, which is useful for directly converting NO3 - wastewater into NH3 [17]. Nowadays, the conversion of NO3 - is not only limited by the low proton transfer efficiency and weak NO3 - adsorption, but also hindered by the competitive hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [1]. Great efforts have been exerted to the development of electrocatalysts that can inhibit HER to obtain high catalytic activity and selectivity [18,19]. However, it is also worth noting that the active hydrogen (H*ads) produced from water splitting is extremely crucial during the process of NO3 - conversion, which can participate in the hydrogenation of the N-containing intermediates and finally convert to NH3 [12]. It is reported that the excessive suppression of HER effectively improves the FE, but it may lead to the insufficient supply of H*ads, resulting in incomplete utilization of NO3 - and lower NH3 yield [20–22]. Recent works show that it is crucial to reconsider the role of generation and consumption of H*ads to enhance the conversion of N-containing intermediates, by various tuning methods of the active site electronic structure. However, simultaneous promotion of the generation of H*ads and its combining with N-containing intermediates is still highly challenging, because of the intrinsically different kinetics requirements on the active sites.

As an emerging carbon material, carbon dots (CDs) are composed of a graphitic core with conjugated sp2/sp3 domain and rich functional groups (-COOH, -OH, -CO, etc.), which have been widely employed in various electrocatalytic reactions, such as water splitting, CO2 reduction and nitrogen fixation reactions towards improved charge transfer and kinetics [23–26]. More importantly, the rich surface functional groups render them great potential on the selective substrate adsorption and conversion in complex electrocatalytic reactions. Here, a CDs modified bornite (CDs/Cu5FeS4) electrocatalyst is successfully fabricated ( Fig. 1a) for NO3RR, in which the introduction of CDs not only inhibited HER but also promoted the production of H*ads, as well as the adsorption of NO3 - on Cu5FeS4 and subsequent hydrogenation reaction. The optimized CDs/Cu5FeS4 not only exhibits an outstanding NH3 yield of 215.19 μmol h−1 cm−2 with a FE of 86.52 %, but also effectively converts NO3 --rich groundwater into NH3. Kinetic isotope experiments (KIE) reveal a decreased KIE value, which suggests that CDs can accelerate the water dissociation and transfer H*ads in a timely manner to participate in NO3RR. In-situ Raman spectroscopy and density functional theory (DFT) calculations show that CDs can promote the hydrogenation of the N-containing intermediates during the NO3RR process. This work not only provides an effective route to enhance the generation and conversion of H*ads through CDs for NO3RR for the first time, but also paves the way of H*ads utilization while inhibiting HER for other challenging reaction.

The CDs were synthesized through a one-step alkali-assisted ultrasonic chemical method [27]. In a typical synthesis, 9 g of glucose was dissolved in 50 ml of deionized water. Then 50 ml of NaOH solution (1 M) was added into the solution of glucose, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 6 h at room temperature. The pH of the obtained solution was adjusted to 7 with HCl, and then an equal volume of ethanol was added into the solution under stirring for 1 h. The Cu5FeS4 were prepared according to our previous report [28]. Typically, 0.18 mmol Fe(acac)3 and 0.55 mmol Cu(acac)2 were dissolved in 30 ml dodecanethiol, in which the Cu5FeS4 were formed by intermittent heating in a three-neck flask. The CDs/Cu5FeS4 composite were obtained by electrostatic adsorption. The specific method is as follows: 100 mg of Cu5FeS4 was dispersed in 10 ml of anhydrous ethanol and then certain amounts of 0.1 mg/ml CDs solution (50, 70, 90 and 110 μl) were added and stirred for 1 h. According to the contents of CDs, the samples were denoted as 5 % CDs/Cu5FeS4, 7 % CDs/Cu5FeS4, 9 % CDs/Cu5FeS4 and 11 % CDs/Cu5FeS4.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) and elemental mapping of the catalysts were obtained by a Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to study the phase and crystal structure of catalysts from 20 to 80° (scan rate: 7.0◦/min) with Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 1.5417 Å) on a D/MAX-2500 diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250X, USA) was used to test the surface valence state of catalysts with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (1283.3 eV). The absorption spectra were obtained though the ultraviolet−visible (UV−vis) spectroscopy on a 2550 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The isotopic labeling experiments were measured by 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) analysis (BRUKERAC-P400, Germany).

All electrochemical tests were conducted on a 760E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai, China) by a three-electrode system, in which Ag/AgCl was used as the reference electrode, graphite rod as the counter electrode and the catalyst-modified carbon paper as the working electrode. All potentials were referenced to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) in terms of E (V vs. RHE) = E (V vs. Ag/AgCl) + 0.198 V + 0.059 × pH. The electrochemical NO3RR measurements were performed in a 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution containing 200 ppm NO3 - (45 mg L−1 NO3 --N) using an H-type electrochemical cell separated by a Nafion 211 membrane. After 1 hour of testing with chronoamperometry, the obtained electrolyte was diluted 20 times with water for color reaction by indophenol blue method, from which the NH3 concentration was analyzed using a UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer.

More details of the chemicals and reagents, the calculation formula of NO3RR, determination of the concentration of N-containing species, 1H NMR measurements, in-situ Raman spectroscopy and DFT computational are shown in the Supporting Information.

The CDs/Cu5FeS4 composite catalyst was prepared by electrostatic adsorption, and the feasibility of electrostatic adsorption between CDs and Cu5FeS4 was verified by zeta potentials (Fig. S1). The TEM and HRTEM images of CDs are shown in Fig. 1b and S2, in which CDs are uniformly distributed with an average size of 3.0 ± 0.5 nm (inset of Fig. 1b) and a lattice fringe of 0.23 nm that corresponds to the (100) plane of graphite [27]. The Raman spectrum (Fig. S3) shows two obvious peaks at 1362 cm−1 and 1578 cm−1 corresponding to the D and G bands of CDs [29]. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum in Fig. S4 displays the peaks around 800, 880, 1047, 1090 and 3305 cm−1, corresponding to C-O, C-H, CC, CO and -OH stretching vibrations, respectively, in which the oxygen-containing functional groups is beneficial for improving catalytic performance [30]. Moreover, the morphology and size of Cu5FeS4 is also characterized by TEM and HRTEM in Fig. 1c and S5, in which Cu5FeS4 has a quasi-spherical shape with an average size of 11.5 ± 1.0 nm (inset of Fig. 1c) and a 0.31 nm lattice fringe that corresponds to the (111) plane of cubic phase Cu5FeS4. Fig. 1d shows the TEM image of CDs/Cu5FeS4 composite, indicating that CDs was successfully constructed on the Cu5FeS4. At the same time, CDs/Cu5FeS4 presents an average size of 11.25 ± 1.0 nm (inset of Fig. 1d) that is similar to pure Cu5FeS4, indicating that the addition of CDs did not alter the morphology and size of Cu5FeS4. And the HRTEM of CDs/Cu5FeS4 in Fig. 1e shows the lattice fringes of 0.23 and 0.31 nm, which correspond to the (100) plane of graphite and the (111) plane of cubic phase Cu5FeS4, further proving the successful preparation of the CDs/Cu5FeS4 composite. High-angle annular dark-field (HAADF)-STEM and corresponding elemental mapping images of CDs/Cu5FeS4 were displayed in Fig. 1f and S6, showing that Cu, Fe, S, C and O elements were uniformly distributed in the CDs/Cu5FeS4 composite.

The constitutions and chemical states of the catalysts were further investigated by XRD, Raman and XPS. As shown in Fig. 1g, the XRD patterns indicate that Cu5FeS4 was successfully synthesized, in which the five apparent peaks at 28.0°, 32.5°, 46.6°, 55.3° and 75.2° correspond to the (111), (200), (220), (311) and (331) crystal planes of cubic Cu5FeS4 (JCPDS No. 73–1667), respectively. Notably, the XRD pattern of CDs/Cu5FeS4 is similar to that of pure Cu5FeS4, which may be due to the low content of CDs [31]. To prove the existence of CDs in the composite, additional evidence was obtained from the Raman spectroscopy. Fig. 1h displays two strong peaks at 1365 and 1580 cm−1 in CDs/Cu5FeS4, which are attributed to the D and G bands of CDs. XPS (Fig. S7a) was used to further get information about the surface chemical states of pure Cu5FeS4 and CDs/Cu5FeS4 [32]. In the high-resolution spectrum of Cu 2p (Fig. 1i), two peaks at 951.24 (2p1/2) and 931.47 eV (2p3/2) are from Cu(I) species of CDs/Cu5FeS4 [5]. As for the Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 1j), the binding energies of Fe 2p centered at 724.69 (2p1/2) and 711.19 eV (2p3/2), and the Fe 2p3/2 satellite peak at 719.1 eV gave the evidence of the existence of Fe3+ in the catalyst [33]. The S 2p spectrum for CDs/Cu5FeS4 (Fig. S7b) displays three peaks, in which the two peaks at 162.81 and 160.81 eV correspond to the 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 of S2-, respectively, and the satellite peak at 168.4 eV further proves the existence of S2- [34]. Compared with the pristine Cu5FeS4, the binding energies of Cu and Fe elements in CDs/Cu5FeS4 have a slight blue shift, which not only unveils the existence of a favourable electron transfer interface, but also indicates the role of CDs on promoting charge transport [35,36]. In addition, the C 1 s pattern (Fig. S7c) shows three peaks centered at 287.6, 286.1, and 287.8 eV, which are indexed to the CC, CO, and OC−O groups, respectively, further indicating the existence of CDs in the composite catalyst. These oxygen-rich functional groups also endow the composite with good conductivity and rich active sites, which is beneficial for improving electron transfer [37]. Therefore, according to the above results, the CDs/Cu5FeS4 composite catalyst was successfully prepared with the expected crystal structure and a CDs-dominated charge transfer interface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activities and selectivity for electrocatalytic NO3RR
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical NO3RR performance was evaluated in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution with different concentrations NO3 -. The concentration of NO3 -, NO2 - and NH3 are detected according to colorimetric methods (Fig. S8-S10) [38,39]. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of CDs, Cu5FeS4 and CDs/Cu5FeS4 composite catalysts were firstly tested in Ar-saturated electrolyte containing 200 ppm NO3 - ( Fig. 2a). The bare CDs exhibit a significantly lower reduction current, while Cu5FeS4 and CDs/Cu5FeS4 show higher current density, indicating that bare CDs has a weak response to NO3RR but can enhance the overall NO3RR performance of CDs/Cu5FeS4 owning to the improved charge transfer and interfacial sites [40]. Moreover, Cu and Fe in Cu5FeS4 can also synergistically promote the adsorption and conversion of NO3 - on the catalyst surface, which is also quite important for the synthesis of NH3 [6]. As shown in Fig. 2b, CDs/Cu5FeS4 shows the maximum NO3 - conversion reaches at 66.30 % within 1.0 hour at −1.3 V (vs. RHE), which is obviously increased compared with Cu5FeS4. In addition, the conversion of NO3 - also significantly increases with the prolongation of reaction time (Fig. S11). Figs. 2c and 2d plot NH3 yield and FE of Cu5FeS4 and CDs/Cu5FeS4 tested from −0.9 to −1.3 V (vs. RHE), respectively. The CDs/Cu5FeS4 shows NH3 yield of 106.15 μmol h−1 cm−2 with a FE of 51.21 % under 200 ppm NO3 -, which are 1.4 and 1.6 times higher than those of pure Cu5FeS4 (75.45 μmol h−1 cm−2, 32.54 %), respectively. Notably, when the potential changed from −1.2 to −1.3 V (vs. RHE), the yield of NH3 was barely increased, but the FE decreased from 51.21 % to 41.33 %, which can be attributed to the enhancement of HER under high potential [41]. In addition, the electrocatalytic NO3RR activity of CDs/Cu5FeS4 is higher than most previously reported electrocatalysts at similar condition (Table S1). The catalyst activity of Cu5FeS4 with different contents of CDs were also investigated in Fig. S12, in which the 9 % CDs/Cu5FeS4 exhibits the best NO3RR activity. And the CDs can effectively promote the conversion of NO3 -, thereby enhancing the yield and FE of NH3. At the same time, the catalytic activity of bare CDs was displayed in Fig. S13, where the conversion rate of NO3 - and the yield of NH3 are extremely low, further illustrating that Cu and Fe in Cu5FeS4 are the key active sites in the composite catalyst, playing a dominant role in the conversion of NO3 --to-NH3. Therefore, the results indicate that the CDs can effectively promote the conversion of NO3 - on Cu5FeS4, thereby enhancing the yield and FE of NH3 of catalyst. Importantly, the NH3 yield under different concentrations of NO3 - was also tested in Fig. 2e. The CDs/Cu5FeS4 exhibited excellent catalytic activity in the range of 200–1000 ppm NO3 -, with a maximum ammonia yield of 215.19 μmol h−1 cm−2 and a FE of 86.52 %. Noticeably, the yield and FE of NH3 decrease at higher NO3 - concentrations (1000 ppm), which is possibly due to the insufficient H*ads supplied for NO3RR in neutral media and the strong competitive HER [42,43]. In addition, stability is another key factor to evaluate the electrocatalysts. The NH3 yields and FEs (Fig. 2f) of CDs/Cu5FeS4 show no obvious decay after 12 cycles of chronoamperometric runs (Fig. S14) at – 1.2 V (vs. RHE). Importantly, the XPS spectra (Fig. S15) and TEM image (Fig. S16) are almost unchanged after the NO3RR test. The above evidences clearly prove the outstanding stability of the CDs/Cu5FeS4 under NO3RR process.

To verify the N source and eliminate the potential NH3 pollution, a series of control experiments were carried out. As shown in Fig. 3a, the LSV curves were performed in a Na2SO4 electrolyte with and without NO3 -, which exhibits a stronger current density response in the presence of NO3 -. In absence of nitrate, a small reductive peak was presented at −0.5 V (vs. RHE), which is attributed to the reduction of a small amount of Cu+ in the Cu5FeS4 to Cu0 in pure Na2SO4 electrolyte [44]. Simultaneously, Fig. 3b and S17 further displayed almost no NH3 detected in 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolytes, showing that the synthesized NH3 comes from NO3 - reduction. The 14N and 15N isotope-labeling experiments were also conducted to further analyze the synthesized NH3 quantitatively by utilizing 14NO3 - and 15NO3 - as the N source, respectively. The NMR spectra in Figs. 3c and 3d show that the intensity of characteristic peaks for 14NH4 + and 15NH4 + are positively correlated with the electrolysis time, which further proves that the NH3 is produced by electrochemical NO3RR rather than environmental pollution. The standard curves (14N and 15N) of isotope-labeling experiments are obtained according to the relative integral area of NH4+/C4H4O4 (maleic acid) in Fig. S18 and S19 from the 1H NMR spectra. In addition, the NH3 yields of 14NH4 + (Fig. S20) and the 15NH4 + (Fig. 3e) calculated by 1H NMR were also compared with indophenol blue method. As shown in Fig. 3f, the NH3 yields obtained by different testing methods is consistent. These control experiments and NMR results not only demonstrate the accuracy of the quantitative analysis methods, but also verify the N source and eliminate the potential NH3 pollution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Direct reduction of simulated groundwater to NH3
ce:section.ce:para: Converting NO3 --rich wastewater into NH3 not only helps solve environmental pollution problems, but also provides an effective pathway for the nitrogen-based fertilizers [45]. Therefore, the NO3 - reduction tests were also conducted in simulated groundwater, in which the CDs/Cu5FeS4 shows great potential for electrochemical reduction of groundwater containing NO3 - to fertilizers. As shown in Fig. 4a, the enhancement of current density in LSV curves indicates that the CDs can significantly improve the NO3 --to-NH3 activity of Cu5FeS4 in simulated groundwater. As expected, there is an obvious current change along with the presence of NO3 - for Cu5FeS4 and CDs/Cu5FeS4 under groundwater (Fig. S21), indicating the NO3RR possibility in groundwater. And the NH3 yield and FE with different potentials were also measured in simulated groundwater. With the potential increase, the NH3 yield in CDs/Cu5FeS4 (Fig. 4b) rapidly increased and reached 56.66 μmol h−1 cm−2 at −1.2 V (vs. RHE), which is 2.15 times higher than pure Cu5FeS4 (26.39 μmol h−1 cm−2), indicating an effective conversion of NO3 - to NH3. Similarly, the FE in Fig. 4c was increased from 13.34 % to 32.43 % with the introduction of CDs. The improvement of the yield and FE proves that CDs can effectively promote the conversion of NO3 - to NH3 even in simulated groundwater. In addition, the selectivity of CDs/Cu5FeS4 towards N in Fig. 4d is above 90 % at different potentials, which significantly higher than that of pure Cu5FeS4. The NH3 yield with different times was also displayed in Fig. 4e, in which the NH3 increases with the extension of reaction time, proving that the CDs/Cu5FeS4 can be used for a long time to convert NO3 - containing groundwater into NH3. Importantly, CDs/Cu5FeS4 also displays an outstanding NO3 - removal efficiency in Fig. 4f, which increases over time, reaching 73 % within 4 h. Meanwhile, 12 h long-term test (Fig. S22) of CDs/Cu5FeS4 with NO3 - groundwater also indicates the excellent stability of the catalyst [46].

Considering the excellent catalytic performance of CDs/Cu5FeS4, we further carried out NO3RR test by using a flow cell to demonstrate the practical application potential. The cathode is composed of CDs/Cu5FeS4 loaded on carbon paper, while the anode is composed of a platinum mesh, which are separated by an anion exchange membrane. All the measurements on the flow cell were performed under Ar atmosphere. The LSV curves with and without NO3 - were measured in Fig. 4g, in which the CDs/Cu5FeS4 displayed strong response to NO3 -. The NO3RR activity were measured in a wide potential range from −0.9 to −1.3 V (vs. RHE) under a flow cell, among which the catalysts exhibit superior NO3RR performance in simulated groundwater containing NO3 - (200 ppm). As shown in Fig. 4h, CDs/Cu5FeS4 displays the highest NH3 yield of 82.04 μmol h−1 cm−1 at −1.2 V (vs. RHE), which are much superior than those in the H-type cell. Additionally, a 24-h continuous electrolysis experiment (Fig. 4i) was also performed in the flow cell reactor, displaying outstanding stability and industrial application potential under groundwater containing NO3 -.

A series of comparative experiments were conducted to understand the catalytic mechanism of CDs/Cu5FeS4. Figs. 5a and 5b show the profiles of NO3 --N, NO2 --N and NH4 +-N concentrations plotted over time for Cu5FeS4 and CDs/Cu5FeS4, respectively, in which the CDs/Cu5FeS4 significantly enhances the conversion of NO3 - and the generation of NH3 compared to Cu5FeS4. Moreover, the introduction of CDs significantly increases the conversion of NO3 - to NO2 - (4.8 ppm of NO2 --N at 45 min), which is also confirmed by the NO2 - selectivity of two samples (Fig. S23). More importantly, CDs/Cu5FeS4 also reduces the concentration of NO2 - more rapidly to NH3 as time goes on. As a result, CDs/Cu5FeS4 has lower NO3 - (7.51 %) and higher NH3 (30.17 %) concentrations compared with Cu5FeS4 (NO3 --N 16.08 % and NH4 +-N 21.08 %) after two hours. It is worth noting that the conversion of NO3 - to NO2 - is the rate-determining step (RDS) for NO3RR on many transition metal-based catalysts, further indicating the key role of CDs on the enhanced NO3RR activity [47].

Additionally, H*ads from water splitting plays a key role during the hydrogenation process of NO3 - to NH3. Here the generation of H*ads was explored by measuring CV curves of the catalysts. As shown in Fig. 5c, the CV curves display two oxidation peaks, corresponding to the oxidation of the absorbed H* and adsorbed H*, respectively, in which the H*ads can diffuse into the lattice of the catalyst to form H*abs. Therefore, H*ads represents the H* on the catalyst surface and H*abs represents the H* in the lattice, among which the adsorbed H* is the key active hydrogen species during the electrocatalytic reaction [48,49]. Importantly, the addition of CDs significantly enhances the adsorption of H*ads on the Cu5FeS4 surface, proving that CDs can significantly promote the production of active hydrogen species. Meanwhile, only one oxidation peak was observed on the CV curve of pure CDs (Fig. S24), which can be attributed to the H*ads [50]. The CV curves in Fig. 5d illustrate that the presence of NO3 - significantly reduces the peak intensity of H*ads while H*abs remains almost unchanged, which indicates H*ads not only can effectively participate in NO3RR, but also further illustrates it is a key active hydrogen species during the reaction process. Besides, the adsorption capacity of CDs for H+ was tested by dialysis method (Fig. S25), which continuously increases with time and ultimately reaches 25 mmol g−1 after 60 minutes, indicating the excellent H+ adsorption capacity of CDs with abundant surface functional groups [51]. Therefore, the above results illustrate that CDs can effectively capture and release the generated H*ads and promote the conversion of NO3 - to NH3.

To illustrate the existence of H*ads and the key role of CDs, quenching and capture experiments of H*ads were carried out. The H*ads radical was quenched by using a specific reagent tert-butanol (TBA). The LSV curves in Fig. 5e show that the current density decreases significantly with the increased TBA content, indicating the positive role of H*ads radicals during NO3RR [52]. Similar to Fig. 3a, a small reductive peak also appears at the same position, further demonstrating the reduction of Cu+ in pure Na2SO4 electrolyte. Moreover, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) is an effective method for verifying the generation of H*ads in the electroreduction process, which is based on immediate reaction of H*ads with dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) added in the solution to generate DMPO-H adducts [53]. As shown in Fig. 5f, the EPR spectra display nine characteristic signals with intensity ratios of 1: 1: 2: 1: 2: 1: 2: 1: 1 without NO3 -, in which the signal of CDs/Cu5FeS4 is significantly stronger than that of Cu5FeS4, indicating the promoted production of H*ads by CDs. Notably, the characteristic signals disappeared with the addition of NO3 -, demonstrating that the generated H*ads can be effectively consumed by the N-intermediates during NO3RR.

Proton transfer is another key factor of NO3RR activity of catalysts, which can determine whether the catalyst can transfer H*ads in a timely manner and participate in NO3RR. LSV curves of the two samples (Fig. 5g and S26) were measured in an electrolyte containing 200 ppm NO3 - with different solvents (pure H2O, pure D2O and a mixture of two ratios). The current density continuously decreases with the increase of D2O, indicating that the break of H-OH bond in H2O with the binding of N species is the RDS of NO3RR on the catalyst [54]. To further explore the contribution of proton transfer rate during NO3RR process, the kinetic isotope effect (KIE) of two catalysts was studied. As shown in Fig. 5h, the NH3 yield of two samples in D2O solvent are both lower compared to H2O solvent, in which the value of KIE (YH/YD) decreases from 2.1 to 1.2 with the addition of CDs, indicating that CDs effectively enhanced the water dissociation rate and proton transfer kinetics [55]. In addition, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with different solvents (H2O and D2O) of the samples (Fig. S27) indicate a significantly higher change in the resistance value of Cu5FeS4 (Δ = 0.50 KΩ) compare to that of CDs/Cu5FeS4 (Δ = 0.06 KΩ), suggesting that CDs can effectively improve the proton transfer rate [56]. The increase in electrochemical active surface area (Fig. S28) and decrease in electrochemical impedance value (Fig. S29) of CDs/Cu5FeS4 in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution containing 200 ppm NO3 - further illustrates that CDs validly boost the intrinsic activity of Cu5FeS4 electrocatalyst [57,58]. Meanwhile, the CV curve of CDs (Fig. S30) in Ar-saturated anhydrous acetonitrile shows several redox peaks, which shows the excellent charge storage capacity of CDs [59]. And the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups -OH in CDs can enhance the hydrophilicity of the catalyst and act as electron donors, promoting the adsorption of NO3 - on the catalyst surface and improving the catalytic activity. Meanwhile, -CO can affect the surface charge and serve as a proton transfer site, thereby enhancing the adsorption of NO3 - and boosting protonation during NO3RR [60,61]. Hence, the above results declare the significant role of the CDs in the proton transport process, which can promote the conversion of NO3 - to NH3 through the effective utilization of H*ads.

To gain a more intuitive understanding of the NO3 - to NH3 process, in-situ electrochemical Raman spectra were measured over Cu5FeS4 and CDs/Cu5FeS4 within the potential range from −0.9 to −1.3 V (vs. RHE). As depicted in Figs. 6a and 6b, the obvious peaks at 982 cm−1 are attributed to SO4 2-, which is widely present in the electrolytes [62]. The peak appearing at 1050 cm−1 belongs to NO3 -, whose intensity decreases with the application of potential, indicating that NO3 - is rapidly converted within the applied potential. Significantly, the absorption intensity of NO3 - on CDs/Cu5FeS4 is obviously stronger than that of Cu5FeS4, indicating that the addition of CDs can effectively enhance the adsorption of NO3 - on the catalyst surface, which is beneficial for subsequent conversion. [63,64]. Meanwhile, the continuous enhancement peaks at 1360 cm−1 and 1593 cm−1 are attributed to NO2 - and NH3, respectively, which illustrate that the catalysts can effectively convert NO3 - into NO2 - intermediate and product NH3. More importantly, the peaks of NO2 - and NH3 are also higher on CDs/Cu5FeS4 than Cu5FeS4, indicating that the introduction of CDs enhances the adsorption capacity of the catalyst for N-containing intermediates and promotes the NH3 yield during the NO3RR process [65–67]. Therefore, the above results imply that the CDs can promote the hydrogenation reactions of N-containing intermediates and the generation of NH3 during NO3RR process.

To better uncover the origin of the high performance of CDs/Cu5FeS4, density functional theory (DFT) calculation was carried out to explore the mechanism of reaction pathway. The conversion of NO3 - to NH3 involves nine steps and eight intermediates (Fig. S31). As shown in Fig. 6c, the Gibbs free-energy diagram reveals that the H*ads adsorption step of *NO3 to *NO3H during the NO3RR process would be thermodynamically spontaneous on the two samples. And then another H*ads attaches to *NO3H and forms H2O and *NO2, which was finally reduced to NH3 through step-by-step protonation [68]. Specifically, the first protonation of *NO3 to *NO3H on Cu5FeS4 is identified as the RDS, which requires an energy barrier of + 0.627 eV. For CDs/Cu5FeS4, the first H*ads adsorption on *NO3 requires an energy barrier of + 0.292 eV, which is 0.335 eV lower than that of pure Cu5FeS4. From the thermodynamic point of view, CDs are able to reduce the adsorption energy of RDS of Cu5FeS4 and thus promote the conversion of NO3 - to NO2 -, enhancing the electrocatalytic NO3RR activity. This conclusion is consistent with the in-situ electrochemical Raman spectroscopy that CDs/Cu5FeS4 is more beneficial to the conversion for N-containing intermediates. Finally, the HER activity of the catalysts was also explored by the LSV curves tested in 0.5 M Na2SO4 and DFT calculations. In Fig. S32, CDs exhibits weak HER activity and significantly inhibited the HER of Cu5FeS4. On the other hand, the Gibbs free energy of H* adsorption can be used to further evaluate the HER performance of the catalysts, in which larger |ΔGH*| indicates lower activity of HER. As shown in Fig. S33, CDs/Cu5FeS4 (0.77 eV) displays a higher ΔGH* compared to that pure Cu5FeS4 (0.54 eV), illustrating that the introduction of CDs can effectively suppress the HER of Cu5FeS4 and thereby promoting more H*ads to participate in the NO3RR [69]. According to the above analyses, a plausible mechanism of CDs/Cu5FeS4 for NO3RR is proposed in Fig. 6d. Here, CDs effectively reduce the adsorption energy of PDS of Cu5FeS4 during NO3RR process, thereby promoting the adsorption of N-containing intermediates and subsequent hydrogenation reactions. Furthermore, the strong charge storage and release capabilities of CDs not only effectively suppress the HER of Cu5FeS4, but also effectively capture and convert H*ads for N-containing intermediates hydrogenation during NO3RR processes.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized novel CDs/Cu5FeS4 electrocatalyst for efficient NO3RR by promoting the generation and conversion of H*ads in hydrogenation reactions. The optimized CDs/Cu5FeS4 catalyst exhibits an excellent NH3 yield of 215.19 μmol h−1 cm−2 with a FE of 86.52 %. More importantly, CDs/Cu5FeS4 can effectively convert the simulated NO3 --rich groundwater into NH3 in a flow cell. The KIE and in-situ H*ads tests indicate that CDs not only promotes the proton transfer kinetics, but also enhances the capture and release of H*ads for N-containing intermediates hydrogenation during NO3RR process. In-situ Raman spectroscopy and DFT calculations reveal that the introduction of CDs can reduce the energy barriers of the NO3RR process and boost the hydrogenation of the N-containing intermediates. This work not only opens a new pathway for designing catalysts, but also emphasizes the key role of CDs as novel H*ads carrier in NO3RR process.
